# Codex App-Server Recovery Design

> Superseded for runtime architecture by
> `2026-07-20-codex-app-server-multiplex-refactor.md`. The MCP rollback transport described below
> was removed; Codex app-server is now the only supported Codex transport.

**Date:** 2026-07-20  
**Status:** Approved  
**Historical baseline:** `53c10ab74edd93ad809fae2088122774bafd4a01`  
**Protocol baseline:** JSON schema generated by installed `codex-cli 0.144.0`

## Goal

Reconnect MAMA's Codex backend through the current Codex app-server protocol while preserving
existing MAMA configuration compatibility, restoring durable thread continuation, and preventing
the per-session `CODEX_HOME` growth that previously consumed tens of gigabytes.

## Context

MAMA previously implemented a `CodexAppServerRuntimeProcess` on two local branches. That runtime
handled app-server initialization, thread and turn requests, streamed response deltas, token usage,
authentication refreshes, instruction-source validation, and denial of native Codex tool requests.
It was never merged into `main`.

The old runtime created both `HOME` and `CODEX_HOME` under a new MAMA session UUID. Resetting or
stopping the runtime cleared in-memory identifiers but never removed those directories. This
duplicated Codex state and left unbounded session artifacts under `~/.mama/codex-app-server/`.

Current `main` uses `codex mcp-server` behind the public `codex-mcp` backend name. Many configuration,
agent-loop, workflow, and UI contracts depend on that public name.

## Considered Approaches

### 1. Keep the MCP transport and only repair session cleanup

This is the smallest change, but it does not meet the requirement to reconnect through the latest
Codex app-server integration surface. It also leaves MAMA on a transport that exposes fewer native
thread lifecycle operations.

### 2. Cherry-pick the historical app-server commits

This restores working code quickly, but also restores the per-session home layout, single in-memory
thread lifecycle, outdated feature flags, and protocol assumptions. The commits predate substantial
changes on `main`, so a whole-commit merge has high integration risk.

### 3. Selectively recover the app-server transport behind the compatible backend contract

This reuses the proven JSON-RPC and safety logic but adapts it to the current `IModelRunner`, shared
Codex home, thread persistence, and generated protocol schema. This is the selected approach.

## Compatibility Boundary

The user-visible backend value remains `codex-mcp` for this recovery. Existing configuration files,
agent definitions, workflow balancing, onboarding, status reporting, and API validation therefore do
not require a broad migration. A new `agent.codex_transport` setting selects `app-server` or `mcp`.
Its default is `app-server`; `mcp` is a temporary, explicit rollback path to the existing transport.

The legacy value `codex` continues to normalize to the same Codex runtime. An app-server failure does
not change `codex_transport` or fall back to MCP. A startup, authentication, schema, resume, or turn
failure must surface as an explicit error.

## Architecture

### App-server transport

Add a focused app-server runner that:

- starts `codex app-server --stdio`;
- sends `initialize` followed by the `initialized` notification;
- starts new threads with `thread/start`;
- resumes registered threads with `thread/resume`;
- starts prompts with `turn/start`;
- assembles `item/agentMessage/delta` events into the existing `PromptResult`;
- records `thread/tokenUsage/updated` usage;
- resolves only on `turn/completed` and rejects explicit failed or interrupted turns;
- declines native command, file-change, and permission approval requests so execution remains under
  MAMA's gateway tool boundary;
- validates app-server-reported instruction sources against MAMA-owned roots;
- rejects malformed JSON-RPC and unknown responses relevant to the active request.

The low-level app-server runner stays serial: one active turn per runner. `CodexRuntimeProcess` routes
each stable session key to its own runner. This preserves concurrency across AgentLoop lanes while
serializing turns within one conversation. `AgentProcessManager` already allocates runners by channel,
and uses the same routing contract.

The adapter retains at most 16 low-level runners and evicts an idle runner after 10 minutes. Both
limits are constructor options so subprocess tests can use small values. Before routing a prompt it
evicts expired idle runners, then evicts the least-recently-used idle runner if capacity is full. If
every retained runner is busy at capacity, the prompt fails with an explicit capacity error. Stopping
an idle runner does not remove its registry record, so its next prompt creates a process and uses
`thread/resume`. One cleanup timer per adapter is unref'd and cancelled by `stop()`.

### Managed Codex environment

All app-server processes use one managed `CODEX_HOME`, defaulting to `~/.mama/.codex`, and one shared
isolated `HOME`, defaulting to `~/.mama/codex-runtime/home`. MAMA no longer creates a home directory
for every session UUID.

Authentication is copied from the user's global Codex home into the managed Codex home using the
existing fingerprint and permission checks. MAMA writes its controlled Codex configuration into the
managed home. The isolated shared `HOME` prevents unrelated user instructions and plugins from being
implicitly loaded.

The managed `config.toml` contains only invariant MAMA defaults and never contains runner-specific
MCP servers. Each runner parses its own `mcpConfigPath` and translates non-secret server fields to
process-local `codex app-server -c mcp_servers.<name>.<field>=<toml-value>` arguments. Arguments are
passed as a spawn array without a shell. MCP environment values are never placed in arguments:
validated names are configured through each server's `env_vars` field and their values are injected
only into the child environment. Two servers requesting different values for the same environment
name cause an explicit pre-spawn error. Errors and debug logs report names but redact values.

This preserves the existing per-agent Code-Act-only MCP configurations without racing on a shared
file. The `mama` MCP entry remains excluded. Invalid server names, commands, arguments, environments,
or TOML values fail before spawn.

Codex's opaque internal session files are not deleted directly. Eliminating per-session home copies
removes the amplification bug without risking corruption of active threads. MAMA-owned registry
records can be pruned independently because they do not contain conversation payloads.

### Stable session routing

Add `sessionKey` to `PromptOptions`. `AgentLoop` passes its stable `source:channelId` channel key on
every prompt; it no longer uses the random SessionPool CLI ID as the Codex registry identity.
`PromptOptions.sessionId` remains the CLI session identifier used by the Claude adapter.

Multi-agent runners receive a stable registry key at construction from their existing channel key.
Calling `sendMessage` without per-call options uses that constructor key. A missing key is an explicit
configuration error; it never falls back to a random UUID or a shared `default` entry.

`resumeSession: false` removes only the selected key's thread mapping before starting the turn.
`resumeSession: true` resumes the mapped thread or starts one only when no mapping exists. A changed
model or working directory on a mapped thread is rejected unless the caller explicitly resets it.

`setSessionId` remains for interface compatibility but sets the adapter's default stable routing key,
not a Codex thread ID. `setSystemPrompt` changes the default prompt for newly created threads; an
existing thread keeps its original instructions unless explicitly reset. Per-call `systemPrompt` is
used when starting a new thread and is rejected when it attempts to mutate an existing thread.
Per-call `requestTimeout` controls that request's timer. Per-call `model` follows the model/reset rule
above. `mcpConfigPath` is validated and translated by a focused process-argument builder that reuses
the existing MCP JSON validation rules; invalid or unsupported configuration is an explicit startup
error.

### Durable thread registry

Add one atomic JSON record per session under
`~/.mama/codex-runtime/threads/<sha256(session-key)>.json`. Each record contains:

- schema version `1`;
- SHA-256 digest of the stable session key, matching its filename;
- the stable session key for collision and mismatch validation;
- Codex thread ID;
- creation and last-used timestamps;
- model and working-directory identity used to validate safe reuse;
- SHA-256 fingerprint of the exact effective system prompt;
- SHA-256 fingerprint of the normalized effective MCP configuration.

The model, resolved working directory, system-prompt fingerprint, and MCP fingerprint form the
immutable thread-policy identity. Empty prompts and empty MCP configurations have explicit stable
fingerprints. Resume rejects any policy mismatch and tells the caller to request an explicit reset;
it never changes a resumed thread's instructions or tool capabilities silently.

On the first prompt for a key, the runner creates a thread and records it. On process restart, it
uses `thread/resume`. An explicit session reset removes the mapping and creates a new thread on the
next prompt. A resume failure is reported and does not silently create a replacement thread.

Writes use a unique temporary sibling file followed by rename. Per-key files avoid lost updates when
different channel runners write concurrently. The in-process lane/runner lock serializes writes to
the same key. Invalid registry content, a hash/key mismatch, or an unexpected record version throws
an explicit error instead of being replaced with defaults.

The registry directory is created with mode `0700`; records and temporary files use `0600`. Existing
paths with broader permissions are tightened before use. Symlinks are rejected for the registry
directory and record path.

Registry records are intentionally not pruned automatically in this recovery. They contain only
small pointers and timestamps; automatic deletion would weaken durable resume and introduce races.
Codex remains responsible for its own session storage.

### Integration

`CodexRuntimeProcess` remains the adapter consumed by `AgentLoop` and `AgentProcessManager`. Its
public backend type stays `codex-mcp` for compatibility. In app-server mode it owns a map of stable
session keys to serial low-level runners. In MCP rollback mode it delegates to the existing
`CodexMCPProcess`. The adapter forwards runner methods and preserves the existing `PromptResult`,
metrics, reset, and health contracts.

The multi-runner adapter contract is:

- `isHealthy()` returns false only after the adapter is stopped or while any retained low-level
  runner is unhealthy. A runner that dies during a prompt is stopped and evicted after rejecting that
  prompt, so a later prompt can create a replacement and resume from the preserved registry record.
- `getMetrics()` is tracked at the adapter boundary and returns total requests, total failures,
  weighted average latency across all keys, and the most recent request timestamp.
- `stop()` marks the adapter dead, stops every low-level runner, rejects their pending work, clears
  the runner map, and is idempotent.
- `getSessionId()` returns the Codex thread ID for the constructor/default routing key, or an empty
  string when that key has not started a thread. It never selects an arbitrary key.
- `setSessionId()` changes only the default routing key used by optionless `sendMessage`; it does not
  mutate per-call routes already in flight.

The current MCP process implementation remains in source as the explicit `codex_transport: mcp`
rollback path. It may be removed in a separately reviewed cleanup after app-server operation has been
verified over time.

Transport selection is validated in `cli/config/types.ts` and `config-manager.ts`, passed through
gateway and agent-loop initialization, carried in `MultiAgentRuntimeOptions`, and consumed by both
the direct `AgentLoop` constructor and `AgentProcessManager` factory. Missing configuration defaults
to `app-server`. Both public backend values `codex` and `codex-mcp` use the selected transport;
`claude` ignores it. An unknown transport value prevents startup.

Configured external MCP servers remain available through the controlled Codex configuration. The
MAMA MCP server is still excluded so the model cannot bypass GatewayToolExecutor. Native command,
file-change, permission, browser, web, app, plugin, and collaboration capabilities stay disabled or
are declined by the client boundary.

## Error Handling

- Spawn and initialization failures leave the runner dead and reject pending requests.
- Authentication failures use the existing Codex authentication classifier.
- Every JSON-RPC request has a bounded timeout and pending requests are rejected on shutdown.
- Process exit rejects the active turn and all requests exactly once.
- A busy runner rejects a second prompt explicitly.
- A thread resume failure preserves the registry record for diagnosis and surfaces the server error.
- Instruction sources outside the managed home or configured workspace terminate the request.
- Native tool requests receive a declined response and do not hang the turn.
- Late or duplicate completion notifications are ignored after the matching turn settles.
- An event for another thread or turn cannot settle the active request.

## Legacy Storage Visibility

Startup checks whether the historical `~/.mama/codex-app-server` root exists and emits one warning
that it is unused and will remain untouched. `mama status` reports its directory count and measured
size so residual growth is visible.

Cleanup is a separate opt-in operation after MAMA is stopped and the user has verified no process is
using that root. This recovery does not automatically delete, migrate, or overwrite legacy session
data.

## Testing

Use a local fake Codex executable and real subprocess I/O. Do not mock MAMA internals.

Required regression coverage:

1. app-server initialization and `initialized` notification;
2. `thread/start`, streamed delta assembly, usage, and `turn/completed`;
3. one shared managed `CODEX_HOME` and shared isolated `HOME` across session keys;
4. durable registry write and `thread/resume` after runner restart;
5. explicit reset creating a new thread without creating a new home;
6. invalid registry rejection;
7. authentication refresh and app-server restart;
8. external instruction-source rejection;
9. native approval request decline without a hang;
10. process exit and timeout cleanup;
11. `AgentProcessManager` factory selecting the app-server adapter;
12. existing `codex-mcp` configuration compatibility;
13. stable channel-key routing independent of random SessionPool IDs;
14. overlapping prompts for different session keys and same-key busy rejection;
15. concurrent registry writes for different keys without lost records;
16. failed and interrupted turns, malformed JSON-RPC, and process exit cleanup;
17. instruction-source validation on both `thread/start` and `thread/resume`;
18. duplicate, late, and cross-thread completion notifications;
19. per-call model, system prompt, timeout, reset, and missing-key contracts;
20. valid and invalid external MCP configuration handling;
21. repeated session creation proving no per-session HOME or CODEX_HOME directories appear;
22. legacy storage detection and status reporting without deletion;
23. process-local full and Code-Act-only MCP configurations without shared-file overwrite;
24. multi-key adapter health, aggregate metrics, stop, eviction/recovery, and default session ID;
25. configuration validation and factory selection for both transports and both Codex aliases;
26. registry version, key digest, key content, permissions, and symlink rejection;
27. idle and LRU runner eviction, capacity rejection, and resume after eviction;
28. system-prompt and MCP-policy fingerprint persistence and mismatch rejection on resume;
29. MCP environment values absent from spawned arguments and logs, with conflicting names rejected.

Run the focused standalone tests first, then standalone type checking and build, followed by the full
root test and build commands. Vitest must retain its single-fork configuration.

## Rollout and Verification

1. Build and test without changing the running backend.
2. Stop MAMA and back up `~/.mama/config.yaml` plus `~/.mama/.codex` metadata.
3. Set `agent.backend: codex-mcp`, `agent.codex_transport: app-server`, and choose a model accepted by
   `model/list` or the configured current Codex model.
4. Start MAMA and verify process status and startup logs.
5. Execute two prompts in the same MAMA session and confirm one Codex thread is reused.
6. Restart MAMA, execute another prompt, and confirm `thread/resume` reuses that thread.
7. Confirm no new directory appears under the removed `~/.mama/codex-app-server/<UUID>` layout.
8. Confirm managed Codex storage remains under the shared roots and report its measured size.

Transport rollback sets `agent.codex_transport: mcp` and restarts MAMA. Full rollback restores the
configuration backup and selects the Claude backend. Neither path deletes Codex state automatically.

If legacy storage is reported, cleanup is performed only after stopping MAMA, resolving the exact
legacy path, recording its size, and receiving explicit user confirmation. Cleanup is not part of
automatic rollout.

## Non-Goals

- Renaming every public `codex-mcp` backend value in this change.
- Multiplexing concurrent turns through one low-level app-server runner.
- Deleting or rewriting Codex's opaque internal session files.
- Enabling Codex native shell, file mutation, web, browser, app, plugin, or collaboration tools.
- Changing MAMA's embedding model, database schema, or MCP server package.
